reasoning: We start with the empirical base rate of 0.422, but heavily weight the FRED statistical model's projection of P(increase) = 0.62, factoring in short-term mean reversion from recent lows (4.38% on Oct 24) and seasonal trends. While the FOMC meets on October 28-29 and is expected to cut rates, such moves are generally already priced into the yield curve. Analysts note that inflation concerns and deficit pressures will likely keep longer-duration Treasury yields elevated (with targets rising from current ~4.0% toward 4.10%-4.17%). Additionally, corporate credit spreads are presently extremely tight, meaning any reversion to the mean in spreads would result in an increase in effective corporate yields. The baseline yield on Oct 24 reached a recent low of 4.38%, suggesting near-term upward resistance is more likely than continued rapid descent. Adjusting the probabilities downwards toward 0.5 over longer horizons per the instructions ('uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon'), the final probabilities are 0.58, 0.55, and 0.52.
